title: Safe Patient Handling and Body Mechanics
subtitle: A nurse must reposition and transfer a weak, partially dependent patient. Using proper body mechanics and safe-handling equipment protects both the patient and the nurse from injury.

patient.name: Safe Patient Handling
patient.admitDate: A weak, partially dependent patient must be repositioned in bed and transferred to a chair. The nurse applies proper body mechanics and safe-handling equipment to prevent injury.

vitals.temp: Protect patient AND nurse from injury
Lifts, slings, draw sheet, gait belt
Back injury risk → never lift with the back

labs.v: Lift with legs, not the back.
Bending at the waist to lift.

nursesNote: A weak, partially dependent patient needs repositioning in bed and a transfer to the chair. Safe handling protects the patient and the nurse: the nurse first assesses the patient's weight, strength, and ability to assist, then uses mechanical lifts, friction-reducing draw sheets, slings, or a gait belt as needed and gets adequate help. Good body mechanics include a wide base of support (feet shoulder-width apart), bending at the knees and hips with the back straight, keeping the load close, pushing or rolling rather than lifting, and pivoting the whole body instead of twisting the spine. The nurse raises the bed to waist height, locks the wheels of the bed and chair, applies a gait belt over clothing, and moves on a count. The nurse never lifts a heavy patient alone or lifts with the back.
providerOrders: Assess weight, strength, and ability to assist before moving the patient
Use mechanical lifts and assistive devices for heavy or dependent patients
Lock the wheels and raise the bed to waist height before transfers
Bend the knees, keep the load close, and avoid twisting the spine
Get adequate help; never lift a heavy patient alone or with the back

opts.text: Bend at the knees and hips while keeping the back straight
Keep the load close to your body
Twist at the waist to move a load quickly to the side
Widen your base of support by spreading your feet
Push or roll a load rather than lifting when possible
Lift a heavy patient alone to save time
Assess weight, strength, and ability to assist before moving the patient
Use mechanical lifts or assistive devices for dependent or heavy patients
Lock the wheels of the bed and chair before a transfer
A gait belt is applied over bare skin under the arms
Raise the bed to waist height to avoid bending and reaching
Manual lifting is always safer than using equipment

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps before a bed-to-chair transfer of a weak patient

opts.text: Assess the patient's strength, weight, and ability to assist
Gather adequate help and the right assistive device
Lock the wheels and position the chair next to the bed
Apply the gait belt and lower the bed to a safe height
Transfer on a count, keeping the patient close
Locking the bed and wheelchair wheels before transfer
Bending at the waist with straight knees to lift
Using a mechanical lift for a fully dependent patient
Twisting the spine while carrying a load
Keeping feet shoulder-width apart for a wide base
Pulling a heavy patient up in bed alone
Raising the bed to waist height before care
Reaching across the bed to lift a patient

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence repositioning a patient up in bed
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the patient and get adequate help or a device
Raise the bed to waist height and lower the head of bed if tolerated
Lock the wheels and place a friction-reducing draw sheet
Face the head of bed, widen your stance, and bend your knees
Shift your weight and move on a count, keeping your back straight
Using assistive devices and asking for help with heavy patients
Lifting with the back instead of the legs
Locking wheels and raising the bed before a transfer
Twisting the body to move a patient quickly
Keeping the load close and pushing rather than pulling
Reaching and bending at the waist to reposition
Applying a gait belt over clothing for ambulation
Transferring a dependent patient alone to save time

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
